bulletins.highlights: The next avalanche bulletin will appear on 11 February at 08.00 h.

avalancheActivity.highlights: Evening and night: High, level 4. Wednesday: Considerable, level 3.
avalancheActivity.comment:
Evening and night: As the penetration by moisture increases medium-sized to large natural wet avalanches are to be expected below approximately 2500 m. In particular very steep shady slopes areas where the snow cover is rather shallow: They can in many cases be triggered in the old snowpack. Individual very large avalanches are further not ruled out. Gliding avalanches can be released at any time of day or night.

Especially high altitudes: In addition dry snow slides and avalanches are to be expected. They can be released very easily or triggered naturally.

The next avalanche bulletin will appear tomorrow, Wednesday, 11 February at 08.00 h.

snowpackStructure.comment:
Tuesday: Already many medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large moist avalanches have been released as the moisture increases. 10 to 15 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall until late morning above approximately 2400 m. Up to intermediate altitudes rain will fall over a wide area. The snowpack will be moist in high Alpine regions.

Wednesday: The weather will be partly cloudy. The surface of the snowpack will only just freeze and will soften during the day. High altitudes: The wind will transport only a little snow.

Large-grained weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack in particular on shady slopes. At intermediate altitudes there are 120 to 200 cm of snow, and even more in some localities.

tendency.highlights:
Wednesday: Gradual decrease in danger of moist avalanches as the temperature drops. From early morning danger level 3 (considerable) will be reached.
Thursday: The avalanche danger will persist.
